content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. ​What is the best "adult-friendly" animated or Pixar film for a couple in their 60s to watch?

**Score:** 50
**Body:** My wife and I are both in our 60s and looking for a movie to watch tonight. We haven't really kept up with modern animation—or "cartoons" as we used to call them—and we aren't looking for anime.

​We want something with a bit of substance that isn't just for kids. We’ve heard Pixar has some great storytelling, but we aren't sure where to start.

​What is the best "adult-friendly" animated film for a couple our age? We appreciate good themes, maybe something regarding life stages, family, or just a really well-told story.

​What should we put on the top of our list?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Up
  - Do you want them to cry?


    - It's Pixar.  That's almost guaranteed regardless of the movie. 
  - That shit hits when you're 20 (or 40 in my case). It must smack really hard as one approaches their golden years. Either way, any movie that can reduce me to tears within minutes of meeting characters with zero dialogue is a winner in my book. 
2. WALL-E
3. Soul is a good one for adults.
4. The Incredibles! 

They are SO married.  Hilarious. 
5. Ratatouille and The Incredibles are two of the more “grown up” ones, different vibes but both great films 
6. The Wild Robot
7. Ratatouille is my pick, especially if you guys are foodies 

## 2. Is there an alien invasion move except its humans that are the invading aliens?

**Score:** 39
**Body:** And i dont mean something like starship trooper or Avatar since thats stil viewed from the side of the humans or there is a human hero. 

i mean it starts on an alien planet with an alien being the main character and the aliens that invade are humans. Is there a movie like that?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Enders game 
2. Planet 51! 
3. >!Extinction (2018) !<\- But knowing that going in wrecks the whole movie.
4. *Enemy Mine* (1985), Dennis Quaid and Lou Gossett Jr. 

If cinematic TV series are okay, there are a few.

*Battlestar Galactica: The Plan* is more from the Cylon's perspective than most episodes.

And because the BSG reboot deliberately blurs the boundaries over which came first – humans or Cylons – through a recursive timeline, the show often invites viewers to be sympathetic to the Cylons. 

And all of *The Expanse* series is told from shifting perspectives – Earth humans, Mars humans, Belters. And the new life forms they encounter, although no episodes really focus on their perspectives.

*The Martian Chronicles* (1980) includes at least one episode from the Martian perspective. Been a few minutes since I watched that show so my memory is fuzzy.
5. Not a movie but a TV episode. >!*Season 2 Episode 15* of The Twilight Zone!<
  - Also Futurama's *War is the H Word*
6. editing post ahaha:  
Avatar  
Starship Troopers

ahem:  
Aliens  
Battle for Terra
  - Damn never heard of those. gues il check them out thanks bud
    - Woah woah woah. You've never heard of *Aliens* ?? You should watch Alien first, cause Aliens is tied for being the best sequel ever made (alongside Terminator 2) 
7. Enemy Mine

## 3. Sick rainy day for my 17 year old daughter.

**Score:** 35
**Body:** What should we watch?

She loves Little Women, 10 things I hate about you, The Sound of Music, Mean Girls, Enola Holmes.

We like a youngish, strong female lead that has some adventure, is witty, academic is good, not too much romance, whimsy, full of dreams, etc.

Go Reddit Go!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maybe...

Ladybird  
A Room with a View  
Spirited Away  
Amelie  
Ever After  
Emma. (2020)  
Marie Antoinette  
Crazy Rich Asians  
A League of Their Own  

  - A League of Their Own is timeless.
  - Ever After for sure! 
2. Pleasantville
3. - Bend It Like Beckham 
- Stick It 
- Saved!
4. Amelie is a great one!
5. Adventures in Babysitting 

Sister Act

Tomb Raider 

Wonder Woman 

Captain Marvel 

Hidden Figures 

A League of Their Own 

Erin Brockovitch 

Fried Green Tomatoes 

6. 1000 votes for Now and Then
7. I just watched the devil wears Prada with my daughter in preparation for the sequel she really liked it also legally blonde she likes too
8. Labyrinth, even though seen as more of a children's movie, still is a great watch imo

## 4. Non-American with an idyllic view of suburbia. What films will shatter it?

**Score:** 29
**Body:** As someone from outside the US, I have always had a picturesque, almost romanticised view of American suburbia. Quiet streets, neat lawns, that sort of thing.

But I know that for many people who actually live there, it is often seen as sterile, isolating, even quietly suffocating.

I am looking for films that dismantle that idealised image and show what is going on beneath the surface. Social tension, boredom, repression, whatever form it takes.

American Beauty was a great example of what I mean.

No David Lynch please. I love his work but I have seen most of it already.

What would you recommend?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The ice storm 

American Beauty 

Far from heaven 

Pleasantville 

  - The Ice Storm is an underappreciated masterpiece. 
2. The Virgin Suicides
3. Heathers
4. American History X
5. Edward scissor hands 
6. The Burbs 
7. Dinner In America 

Edward Scissorhands

Suburbia (Penelope Spheeris, 1983)

Pump Up The Volume

Heathers

Mean Girls

Over The Edge (1979)

i guess the common thread here is movies about an outsider who doesn't have a neat place in the social order of polite suburbia. mostly kids and punks in these cases.

The Addams Family is a version that plays it for laughs.
8. Revolutionary Road. And read the book. 
9. Brick (2005)

## 5. Movies similar to The Man From Earth

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Looking for movies like The Man From Earth. Something revolving around religion, god, science, history and stuff like that.

Needs to be dialogue heavy and brownie points if it's set in a single location. 

Any language works as long as it checks all the boxes 

Thank you in advance 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Sunset Limited (2011)
2. My Dinner with Andre is dialogue heavy single location with two friends discussing life. 

Man From Earth also has a sequel but it’s hot garbage 
  - Everyone shits on the sequel and I agree its not as good, but I think it does a decent job exploring what John's life has probably been like.   If it was more of the same people would still hate it.
    - Yeah that's true, it has a few moments that's good. It's just that the second half turns into a generic movie, imo, but it does have some interesting moments. 
      - It does kind of unravel, doesnt it?   I really would have liked to have a prequel instead, just pick any historical era and drop him in with thousands of years of human knowledge and show how he blends in.
3. The Seventh Seal (1957)

Stalker (1979)

Waking Life (2001)
4. Creator with Peter O’Toole. An oldie but goodie.
5. Holes (2003)
6. Mind Walk (1990)
7. Freud's Last Session (2023) - C.S. Lewis vs Freud arguing in one room  
Mindwalk (1990) - long walk through Mont Saint-Michel, science and meaning

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Was Daniel Craig the most perfectly cast James Bond ever?

**Score:** 2053

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pierce in Goldeneye was absolute perfection 
  - He embodies what James Bond’s charisma and looks should be just not best movie scripts 
    - This. The problems with the post-Goldeneye films had nothing to do with Brosnan.
      - Brosnan was shortchanged, absolutely.
  - Upvoted and agree, but he was of a time. Pierce was the perfect punctuation at the end of an era of tongue in cheek, debonair, suave secret agents, with over the top gadgets and a sprinkling of womanising (though Brosnan toned down the sexual assault level of flirting). He was the poetic manifestation of standards set by those before him. Brilliant. 

A "Daniel Craig" Bond was necessary to keep the idea of Bond alive in a progressive 21st Century society. He set a new standard that will be hard to beat. 
2. He was more than controversial at the time of Casino Royale announcement. As I remember, not many people really believed in him. He proved them wrong and everyone loved him eventually. 

Before 2006 consensus was that Brosnan was as perfect casting as it could be 
  - >Before 2006 consensus was that Brosnan was as perfect casting as it could be

I still think Brosnan was peak casting, but Craig's movies are just of a much higher quality.
3. I mean, I think they are all really well cast for what they wanted from the movies. Craig brought the humanity,  Dalton being the intensity, Brosnan brings  charm, Connery brought the gravitas and Moore brought the fun…. And Lazenby just brought … a rip off of Connery 😂
  - In fairness to Lazenby, the concept of recasting Bond was completely new, and trying to make it as gentle a transition as possible was a fair judgment call. 

(Just relistened to the OHMSS episode of From Rewatch With Love, hugely recommend)
4. ![gif](giphy|5Gy20UUza3tTi)

## 2. That one favourite scene where the director makes you believe something for a moment, but then completely flips it. My pick: the police car scene at the end of Get Out

**Score:** 1143

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. classic example is Silence of the Lambs. Demme makes you think the police are closing in on Buffalo Bill only to reveal that they’re at a dead end and Clarice is in a heap of danger. 
  - The 2003 Texas Chainsaw Massacre remake is a mixed bag but R. Lee Ermey nailed playing Hoyt. Just made his character into the most disgusting and deplorable human being. You wanted to see him get his comeuppance.




Later in the movie the protagonist is trying to escape and is seen entering a vehicle. As she is trying to hot wire it we got Hoyt searching for her. We think he is about to enter the truck she is hot wiring. And right as the door opens to reveal her, shes not there, he steps back and then it cuts to her running him over with a different car. The theater went absolutely nuts.
2. he mist finale still haunts me, it convinces you there’s no hope left and then immediately proves you wrong in the worst possible way
  - Even Stephen king said he wished he’d come up with that ending
3. TS MOTHAFUCKIN A
4. “The Prestige” did this absolutely amazingly
5. I read somewhere that the original ending had it be actual police and he got arrested, but it was written while Obama was president and Peele wanted to show people that not everything was fixed just because the USA had a blsck president. However by the time they were looking at releasing the film Trump was in office and he changed his mind and figured at this point people just needed a win. 
  - I actually love that it subverts what you’d expect, that he’s going to get arrested and blamed for everything, or even getting shot. Everything points to this having a tragic ending for him but it doesn’t.
6. Watchmen ending - The guy is monologuing and you think that it gives the "heroes" a chance to stop him, until he reveals that he released the information to the world 35 minutes earlier, framing Dr Manhattan.

  
Changed 15 minutes to 35 - Thank you BumpyNubbins for the correction.
7. I mean, I told you not to go in that house...

## 3. What’s the weirdest way a character died in a movie?

**Score:** 871
**Body:** I’ll start. Admiral Chuck Farrell in GoldenEye. Dude got laid only to be crushed by Xienna’s thighs, and when Bond came across his corpse, it fell out of a closet and even more weird is he had a happy-like expression on his face 😳

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|37H5XhwrXuHPq)
2. ![gif](giphy|Wsk4ZbGMeavNm)

Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery
  - Fun fact, that actor, Michael McDonald, is the only actor to be killed on screen by both Mike Myers and Michael Myers

https://preview.redd.it/5uofbg1xjjyg1.jpeg?width=1200&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a0e5df9d56515371c3ebe9ceec29e60ebf331f1d


3. ![gif](giphy|11Nd2bbZjrQrO8)

“There goes my hero!”
4. https://i.redd.it/jeu0cxjh9jyg1.gif

Also from Goldeneye “I am invincible!”
5. ![gif](giphy|xUA7b3DVGl7ehPISis|downsized)
6. The weirdest one I've ever seen is actually on television. I think it's the first episode of American Gods where the guy gets swallowed by a vagina. 
7. Dr strangelove

![gif](giphy|5eM4x8fxZNzPO)
8. My corpse would look like the fucking Joker if Xenia crushed me to death with her thighs. I'm not sure what OP is on about. 
  - first off, your name 

secondly, lol imagine crime scene investigators 

“Bob, that dead man appears to have a rictus grin on his face.”

“*sigh* It’s Xienna again.”

## 4. 🎬 Oppenheimer

**Score:** 588

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Well, i saw Barbie with my mates at my 50 year birthday. It was glorious, i am quite sure that we 3 middleaged dudes raised the average age in the cinema by at least 10 years. 
2. This is obviously just a joke 
3. I really enjoyed Barbie. I was gonna watch Oppenheimer but I heard it was kind of a slog for a 3+ hour movie
  - Watch Barbie again instead. It gives you a better understanding of the world than Oppenheimer does. 👍
    - wait, what new understanding of the world did you gain from Barbie ? 
      - We're all going to die
4. To be fair, if it's a Nolan movie, you don't even need to pay attention.

A character or the narrator will explain what just happened.
5. She looks like Joy from My name is Earl.
  - ![gif](giphy|xT1XGKzPFenowInxsI)

If they did the live-action 20 years back, Jaime Pressly (aka Joy) probably would have been on the short list for Barbie. She definitely had the comedy chops with stuff like Not Another Teen Movie, and her smaller roles in Can't Hardly Wait & in Tomcats. But My Name is Earl was definitely a showcase for her talent.

She got to star in Mom alongside Anna Faris and Allison Janney, which is also pretty great.
  - Welcome to the internet!

## 5. Macon Blair in Blue Ruin: One of the best normal-guy-turned-assassin performances of all time.

**Score:** 313
**Body:** The revenge genre has a tendency to cast for muscular, smooth-talking archetypes, and instead in *Blue Ruin*, Macon Blair brings a refreshingly plain, dud-like energy. I love it. It's perfect. Who else has turned in a performance like this?

I run a [newsletter](https://www.tuesdaynightmovienight.com/) where we recommend one good movie every week, and we actually went to Sundance this past year. We saw Macon Blair in person during his premiere for a new film he directed starring Dave Franco and O'Shea Jackson Jr. The movie was originally titled *The* *S\*\*\*heads*, but will be distributed as *Idiots*. Anyways, Macon Blair seems like a genuinely great guy, and the film was super well-directed and very, very funny. I can't wait to see how his career unfolds.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great movie. Loved the opening scene. 
2. I especially loved how "reality ensues" the movie was. He's angry, he's determined, but he's borderline inept with his vengeance. I wholeheartedly agree, it was a refreshing movie with great down-to-earth performances. I frequently recommend this movie and Green Room to anyone who will listen.
  - I actually thought it was a realistic portrayal of how an intelligent guy would do in that situation. He’s not inept at all, he has decent strategies, but he’s still just a normal person with no actual training, wanting revenge doesn’t suddenly turn you into John Wick. 
3. Fantastic film. Dwight’s sense of loss and destitution is palpable. Sam’s (sister) simultaneous anger at Dwight and satisfaction in his vengeance feels very realistic to me. Ben’s (ex-military friend) completely unjudgemental help and haven is touching and I think anchors the film. 	
4. I watched this movie recently, and thought the 2nd lead looked familiar. Looked him on IMDB and he was Buzz from Home Alone.
  - What?! I’ve see this movies multiple times and never realized. 
5. Added to watchlist ✅ 
  - You'll enjoy it. Hidden gem of a film.
  - Also, add the Green Room while you are at it. Great back to back films. 
6. I watched this out of nowhere back in the day, spent years trying to remember it, and found it a while back again. 

Really good movie, a good portrayal of how far grief takes you, how revenge doesnt always make thinga right again, and how things get out of control very fast.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Spring (2014) romantic horror filmed beautifully in gorgeous Italy

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I adore horror. Really a genre that can make me feel the largest ranges of emotions, laugh, cry and be white knuckled. Little have I thought of this genre meshed with romance. Spring does this very well and is unique. If I could tell a film person such as yourself what it's like, it's like the before trilogy with body horror. 

Spring is to be remembered by it's two leads who have great chemistry and do very well with their parts. Italy and the coast is another character and the filming takes full advantage of how beautiful Italy really is. There is a tracked shot that plays in slow motion over arpeggios and it’s when we are introduced to our female lead, the woman, the mystery, what is she? We unravel it with our lead Evan and fall in love with this mystery. It’s a very pretty introduction to our second lead. You’ll know when it starts now so you can appreciate it. Modern horror tends to lead to mental illness is the real monster trope, this film is not like that, there is a real monster in the film.

Spring is a film that starts out bleak and gives you a gut punch with the lead's dying mother and how much you can tell he loves her. He goes nuts and has an unfortunate night with a person who had bad intentions. He says fuck it I am going to go to a random place on Earth to hide. It's Italy and he meets a woman who changes his entire path forward. It doesn't do it justice, my summary. It's a horror film you watch with your significant other and both of you will apreciate it. 

This is my recomendation and if it had not moved me I would not type this out for you. I can full heartedly recommend this unknown horror flick. I hope that if you do watch it, that it moves you too.

Edit: horror buffs will remember the actor who plays our lead from the Evil Dead (2013), the dude who gets nails shot into him too many times and more recently from Abigail. If you want a similar romantic horror film check out the film Honeymoon of the same year.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This film often gets overlooked in conversations around "Elevated horror", but I think it's one of the best examples. Elevated horror as a genre can suffer from over-intellectualising at the expense of emotional depth, whereas Spring feels like the director took a premise that could easily have been a joke ("what if David Cronenburg had directed Before Sunrise?") and made something surprisingly warm and moving. The only other horror/horror-adjacent film I can think of where I was similarly invested in the romance was Spoorloos.

I think you could maybe accuse it of wearing its influences on its sleeve a bit too much, to the point where it sometimes feels like it's in Linklater's shadow, but it's still well worth watching in its own right.

## 2. Films where the score feels like a character?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Been thinking a lot lately about films where the score or even just specific sound design choices feel like they're doing more than setting a mood. Like, the music itself has agency, or tells its own parallel story. It's not just background, it's a character in its own right, influencing the narrative. What films come to mind when you think about that level of integration?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Suspiria! The score by Goblin is eerie and often very intrusive - it also tells you what is going on before the film does. It definitely felt less like background and more like a spectator warning you of what was to come. 
2. Birdman. Not watched it in a long while but I always thought the way it was shot overshadowed how wild a choice a drum-only score is. For much of that film it seems that Michael Keaton’s character is being driven by something outside of his control and the drumming really feels like it drives the tone of so many scenes rather than merely reflecting it.
3. Sticking to non-diegetic music.

Jaws. The shark theme is the shark's offscreen body, giving agency to the monster we rarely see. 

The Third Man. The zither score feels like the mocking, amoral, alien intelligence of Harry Lime's Vienna.
4. My go to answer for this is the original Cape Fear (1962) by Bernard Herrmann. 

It is a dark, suspenseful and adds so much tension in this thriller and emphasises the conflict between Max Cady (Robert Mitchum) and the family of Sam Bowden (Gregory Peck).

The score is not regarded as iconic as Herrmann’s best known scores like Psycho and North by Northwest but it perhaps adds more than any of them to the character of a film.
5. I'm not sure I understand how a score would influence the narrative, but there are a few I've thought about a lot lately:

The Burbs - has an iconic Jerry Goldsmith score that feels he's parodying his own past work

The Long Goodbye - John Williams uses the same song throughout the movie. It changes with the scene in really interesting ways.
6. This is an unusual prompt but I will try my best.

Original scores: 

* The Good, The Bad, and the Ugly: Morricone's soundtrack has a clear progression throughout the film, kind of hinting at bigger things, gradually building up... so by the time we are at the standoff with the horn in The Trio blaring it isn't just a cool soundtrack moment, it's a catharsis to the 2.5 hours of soundtrack leading up to that point.

* The Studio Ghibli films: Joe Hisaishi's original scores in my opinion are parallel characters in their own right.

* Sicario: Absolute despair. But it doesn't just set the mood - it fundamentally changes the film. And not just in a 'look at this Harry Potter scene with and without music and see the crazy difference' kind of way - the Sicario score is ominously communicating the themes of the movie even where what you are seeing on the screen explicitly isn't (e.g. shots of mundane scenes while the music tells you that things are very wrong).

* A weaker example because I watched it 15 years ago and didn't particularly love the movie, but I remember the Chemical Brothers' score of the movie Hanna was pretty special. I don't recall it fits this prompt though or if it was more like the Matrix where the soundtrack was just a banger.

Soundtracks:

* Guardians of the Galaxy:  Awesome Mix Vol 1 is not just the soundtrack but is actually involved in the plot too. 

* Baby Driver: beyond the score being quite plot-relevant, so much of the movie (footsteps, character movements, editing) lines up with the beats.

* Most Guy Ritchie crime movies - Lock Stock, Snatch, RocknRolla, The Gentlemen: The song choices are so deliberate and intentional that it almost takes you out of any immersion so that you can appreciate the director saying 'look doesn't this song totally match the vibe of this scene!' but he and the actors are clearly having fun with it so you do too.

* The Life Aquatic: Similar reason to the Guy Ritchie explanation, but somehow both sillier and sadder. The character playing Portuguese covers of David Bowie songs for both humour and sorrow, the use of music by Sigur Rós and The Zombies to communicate grief (for his friend, for his career) and the more whimsical OST when they are on adventures. It is just such a unique and intentional vibe that I think is so deliberate and intentional that it does act as a unique character, too.

TV:

* Sorry one last one: the tv show Fargo's season 3 episode 4, where the episode opens with the villain from season 1 narrating 'Peter and the Wolf' to introduce each character in a way that aligns with both broader themes from the season and leitmotifs used within that episode. It is just so exceedingly creative that it transcends just being a good score.
7. Off the top of my head I’d say Once Upon a Time in the West might fit that bill for me. There’s a tiny bit of diegetic music during the opening scene from a harmonica but it’s not until a pivotal moment maybe 20-30 minutes in that the score introduces itself and it hits like a truck.

>!The first time that music plays is also the first time you’re introduced to the gleeful cruelty of the film’s villain. Cruelty which previously engendered another character’s sole motivation throughout the film, even if that isn’t something that becomes clear to the audience until the ending. !<

>!Also I don’t know if the choice of an electric guitar was intentional or coincidental but to me it really represents the idea that the violence being committed by the villain isn’t purely cruelty, nor the dispassionate calculus of survival. It’s an oddly anachronistic, oppressive and harsh instrument representing an old-fashioned outlaw’s attempt at transforming himself into a modern-day robber baron. It’s excess and the big-business interests of the 1900s arriving to put an end to the relatively modest frontier mindset of the old west. !<
8. I'm not sure what you exactly mean. Can you provide your own examples?

I'm thinking of something like Fantastic Planet or Sweet Sweetback's Badass Song. If anything, these films are often more known for their OSTs, or where they were sampled. The score is a huge part of the appeal.

Would the main themes in Midnight Cowboy or Taxi Driver count as feeling like a character? Or something like August In The Water? Or Shuji Terayama's Grass Labyrinth?
9. Magnolia. Not the Aimee Mann songs, the score by Jon Brion. It's relentless - there in the background for most of the film. It's worth noting that Magnolia is a film where the scenes without scoring stand out. Rather than the conventional method of using occasional music to heighten particular scenes, PTA here uses its occasional absence. 

The music itself is plodding, in strict 4/4 meter, like a metronome marking out the inevitable progress of time itself. PTA said (I think?) he wanted Magnolia to be at the emotional level of opera, and it is. But this relentless scoring, in contrast, is not remotely operatic. It's often quirky, almost humorous - the kind of thing you'd expect to accompany a children's film about magical goblins. It sits completely at odds with the high melodrama of the action. 

The score remains constant when we move from scene to scene (even clashing at times with diegetic music from radios and juke boxes), communicating that there's some meta narrative happening, drawing our attention away from the specifics of each character's story, and towards the bigger, overriding context. The characters are locked into something bigger than they could possibly understand - a machine like inevitability hangs over them. They're like puppets at the service of the machine. The music sounds like clockwork; it's the mechanical parts whirring and locking together in time. 

People talk about Magnolia a lot, but they rarely notice how intrinsic the score is to the success of the film.
10. Three Colours: Blue is the first film where I felt the music was a fully-fledged character. I don't want to give any spoilers, but the music plays an integral part of the plot. The non-diegetic score almost interacts with the main character in a way that doesn't feel contrived or artificial. Music also plays an important part in Kieslowski's The Double Life of Veronique.

Joker, even though I know it has many detractors, had incredibly original and inventive use of score. It doesn't just signal to the audience the feelings of the main character, it's almost as if he can hear the music. The film wouldn't be nearly as effective without Hildur Guðnadóttir score.

## 3. Films that portray the psychological effects of imprisonment, involuntary confinement, or being held hostage?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Hello fellow film lovers. I’m looking for examples of films that really show the psychological impacts of situations where a person is held against their will. Obvious examples include The Deer Hunter, Prisoners  and even the end of A Clockwork Orange. 

They can be about prison, mental health facilities, abduction, hostage situations, really anything where someone is held against their will. 

Films that especially feature cinematography that enhances the effects would be great to hear too. 

Thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Room with Brie Larson and Jacob Tremblay immediately comes to mind. A lot of it is from the perspective of the little boy, but we see the psychological impact of the whole ordeal on both the characters. Amazing performances too.
2. Park Chan-Wook's Old Boy (2003) is an fun one that fits. The main character is imprisoned for 15 years and has no idea who put him there or why. Those 15 years shape his actions and personality once he's freed. 
3. swept away (1974) - i didn't know what to think of this one, but it certainly deals with your themes

scum (1979) - takes place at a british reform school/prison type of place. bleak, realistic, and often ugly

surrender dorothy (1998) - uncomfortable art movie. made me question why i'm more horrified by male sexual victimization than female sexual victimization in movies

beyond the black rainbow (2010) - psychedelic, though not difficult to understand. really beautiful cinematography and sets

chained (2012) - excellent acting, distressing atmosphere. has a pretty big twist so don't search for it beforehand. made by david lynch's daughter

heretic (2024) - some people don't think it's that good and i definitely have some qualms with it, but there is focus on the tension felt by the captor as well as the captives, which i thought was interesting
4. I finally watched Papillon recently, which happens to have a sequence in which the MC does a stretch in solitary confinement. I thought it was particularly affecting, and will possibly remain the most memorable part of the whole 2 1/2 hour runtime.
5. Das Experiment - A German Film based on The Stanford Prison Experiement.

Midnight Express - Based on a Turkish Prison, and based on a true story. 

Both different, and worth the watch!
6. Hard to believe no one has mentioned The Shawshank Redemption.

Additional exposition for sake of the word minimum: a film that is in the conversation for greatest films of all time, that deals deeply with the theme of maintaining humanity in the face of incarceration. Hopefully that is enough words.
7. My recommendation is hunger (2008). It’s about Bobby Sand’s hunger strike in Ireland. It’s a very bleak and disturbing film, as it should be, and I don’t think I’m going to be watching it again any time soon. 
8. It’s not a film but there’s a series called when they see us depicting the Central Park 5 story. I think it’s episode 3 that centers around the eldest boy. It is absolutely haunting. I couldn’t stop thinking about it for weeks after. The actor won an Emmy for it. 
9. The first ones that come to mine for me are Hunger, by Steve McQueen, and Dogtooth, by Yorgos Lanthamos.

People kind of forget to talk about Dogtooth’s depiction of what is very clearly a high control patriarchal cult, and the entirety of the film is the psychological damage on the young women who grow up in a family of processed and managed information and control. It’s right up the alley of what you’re talking about. 
10. The Killing Fields, based on the true story of Dith Pran, a journalist who endured horrific abuse at the hands of the Khmer Rouge, and his eventual escape. It’s a very difficult film to watch but it’s really well done and Haing S. Ngor is outstanding as Pran. Ngor himself had been a prisoner in the Cambodian prison camps before he escaped.

1. I’ve seen it. The director’s cut, it must have been. It has an intermission.

It’s somewhat difficult to remember. It’s a film about a student researcher or young man with an obsession about an earlier Japanese figure, a political free thinker. Like a unique and interesting but forgotten and uninfluential in public life theorist, whose influence was really over a few similarly free thinking women, who to some extent served him. It really seems like he didn’t do or achieve very much. But in the late sixties moment, he’s interesting to this particular student.

I don’t remember whether the film is half and half historical recreation of the life of the earlier figure and of the young man who’s researching him’s day to day life in late sixties Tokyo, or some other city, or whether it’s weighted significantly more heavily towards the period stuff. There is more focus on the historical figure, though. He’s the more notable character. But in the three and a half hours, there are long scenes of the young student living life, having some sexual experiences, etc. Juxtaposed with and in conversation with his obsession, the earlier free thinker.

Anyway the film isn’t the most standardly structured story. I don’t know if that qualifies it as experimental. The very beginning of the film is a funny / weird / jarring introduction, but I wouldn’t call it consistently experimental. 

It’s no more or less experimental than for instance the film Center Stage, the film with Maggie Cheung as the early Hong Kong star Lingyu Rian, where intermittently within the film itself, Cheung and its director, as themselves, speculate on, or excavate the meaning of events in the life of the character Cheung is also playing / portraying.

The 1960s portions of Eros + Massacre are loose and freewheeling but in a fairly standard New Wave kind of way.

2. > Without spoiling anything, what are some of the political themes of the movie?

The underlying message of the film is that the infamous killing of early twentieth century Japanese anarchist Ōsugi Sakae proceeded inextricably from the fact that he had the hots for particular women. The director is interested in that rebel from half a century earlier largely for this reason: love and death. This, along with its nouvelle vague editing techniques, makes it a very Sixties film. Otherwise, politics are not particularly overt.

Definitely watch the director’s cut. If you are used to art cinema, and longish films like those of Jacques Rivette, then the film may not come across as so long that it needs an intermission.

## 5. Fourth Wall Breaks as a Storytelling Tool - Man Bites Dog (1992), Close-Up (1990), and The Holy Mountain (1973)

**Score:** 5
**Body:** The majority of films fashion themselves in such a way to reach maximal immersion. It is often said that the technical aspects of filmmaking are at their best, when they are so well-integrated that the audience doesn’t even realize they are present. Rather, they prop up the story in a way with the most “lubrication” possible, so that the story can occur more naturally to the audience.

It is because of this very common sentiment, that I find it so interesting when films explicitly remind the audience that they are indeed watching a film. When the story that is being told, is elevated by the viewer being ripped away from a distant point of comfort. Not in terms of a character breaking the fourth wall directly, but rather when the facade of the technicalities of filmmaking are dissolved and the viewer is left to remember that what they are watching, is a creation of fallible humans. I’ll use this post to discuss three films which use this storytelling technique in very interesting, very different ways.

————————————-

**Man Bites Dog (1992)** is a French student-made black comedy mockumentary, following a serial killer and his crimes. The nature of mockumentaries already embody this concept I’m talking about, but I think this film really takes it up to another level. From the very start, viewers will feel the absurdity of documentarians shooting murders as if they are blasé occurrences of a profession. We understand that by being there, by not contacting the authorities, the documentarians become complicit in these crimes. This tension, played mostly for humor in the first half of the film, ramps up drastically towards many scenes in the latter half being absolutely brutal to watch, eliminating any possible empathy the audience has for the documentarians.

One exceptional usage of the technique I am talking about here, is a scene taking place in a factory. The crew are searching for a misplaced item, and the shot focuses on the killer monologuing. Interestingly, we can not hear him, and only hear footsteps. Soon, we see the sound guy appear, and the audio of the killer slowly coming into focus. In this scene, the ramblings of a madman were only given meaning by the choice of people to listen to him.

This draws to line the central question of this film - how are audiences complicit in violence when we support a hyper-violent media landscape? Critics of hyper-violence may criticize filmmakers for showcasing such acts of violence. Man Bites Dog asks the question, if filmmakers offer meaning to violence, do film viewers then codify that meaning in society?

————————————-

**Close-Up (1990)** is an Iranian documentary which follows a man who fraudulently claims to be a known Iranian director to ingratiate himself towards a wealthy family, and the subsequent court case. Shot in a fascinating way, the film features direct courtroom footage, alongside reenactments of events played by the very same people involved in the real world.

This dichotomy draws fascinating questions on the nature of documentaries. What is it, exactly, that differentiates a film from a documentary? It seems as if Close-Up is in a way, arguing that there may not be a difference. That, ontologically speaking, you are documenting whenever you are pointing a camera at something, whether the subjects are “acting” or not.

This storytelling technique is taken to its apex at the climax of the film, where the impersonator meets the director he was impersonating. Shot from far off, the scene feels as if we are a distant observer intruding on a moment of privacy. As they first meet, their microphones glitch in and out, and we can only hear very brief moments of their conversation, less than whole words at a time. The filmmakers mutter over the technical problems, despite this being an artistic decision made in post.

The effect this has on the film is so deeply sublime. It feels like the apex of the impersonators entire life, and his first true moment of validation. As if the moment was so pure and so personal to him, that God himself reached down to mess with the audio equipment, so that that moment could only belong to him.

————————————-

These first two films, being mockumentaries and documentaries respectively, are already “self-aware” that they themselves are films. What might this type of storytelling look like in a non-documentary style film? For this, I will discuss **The Holy Mountain (1973)**, and with it, perhaps the most iconic fourth wall break in all of cinema. This film is a kaleidoscopic visual parable on the nature of spirituality and the pitfalls of humanity. Largely eschewing traditional plot, it conveys meaning through extremely stylized visual metaphor.

In the final moments of this experience, jist before our cast summits The Holy Mountain, the lead character (who is also the director, Alejandro Jodorowsky) instructs the camera to zoom back, revealing the nature of the film set.

Here, Jodorowsky doubles down on the importance of the symbolism in this film. He directly reminds the audience that the film is not the real world. By removing the catharsis of a traditional ending, Jodorowsky makes the point that the true conclusion to The Holy Mountain, is the audience embodying its’ themes in real life. By reminding us that it is a film, Jodorowsky also reminds us that is in fact not a film, but a man opening up very deep parts of his psyche for the world to see, and hopefully learn from.

————————————-

I would love to hear your guys’ thoughts on these films, or any other films which do these “technical fourth wall breaks”. I also might liken what I’m talking about to The House of Leaves. Where the media takes advantage of the audiences familiarity with a medium and uses it against them to form something new and emergent.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think fourth-wall-breaks can be excellent storytelling and thematic tools! I kind of wish we'd see them (slightly) more often! 

Blade Runner 2049 contains two of what I think are fourth-wall-breaks, aimed at the then 35-year-on meta-discussion about the nature of Deckard, and I think both are aimed at re-ambiguating and *not* answering the central question. 

**Is Deckard a replicant?**

In the original novel, it's somewhat ambiguous, but I think most readers lean toward "no." In the first film and especially the different cuts, Ridley Scott gives a more concrete "yes," while Harrison Ford has more strongly been on the "no" end. Fans have debated for 35+ years. 

Then we get 2049 Deckard. He's aged, and he's had a daughter. The villain seems to believe that he or his daughter may unlock the secret of replicant reproduction. However, in his first conversation with K after the initial fight, he seems to speak almost directly toward this decades-long meta-discussion:

*Deckard pours some whiskey for his dog companion.*

K: "is *it* real?

Deckard, possibly annoyed by the question: "I don't know, ask *him*."

It's a bit of an inane question, the concern over whether the dog is real is hardly important, and Deckard doesn't seem to care. The answer is as facetious as the question deserves. You can't really ask the dog the question; he won't understand and won't be able to answer. Even if he could, what would his answer be? Then there's the part I believe is there, Deckard/Harrison Ford is perhaps suggesting to the audience: ask him.

That alone I thought was probably a stretch, until I got to the end and the dialogue seemed to address the meta-discussion yet again, as Joe hands Deckard the wooden horse figurine:

Deckard: "Why? What am I to you?" 

K, not answering: "Go meet your daughter." 

If you were aware of or participated in the Deckard debate, this line almost certainly put that right at the forefront of your mind. What is Deckard *to the viewer*. Joe, to Deckard's question, and the film to the meta-question, doesn't answer. And in not answering the question, I feel as if it gave the best possible answer of any I've seen: does it really matter? Being a replicant wouldn't make him not a father, being human wouldn't make him less cold and amoral. He's a complex being with thoughts, feelings, relationships, etc.


  - That’s a super fascinating example, and the concept of a fourth wall break to re-ambiguate is an awesomely clever employment of this technique. It kind of subverts the traditional role of the fourth wall break, which is to put the audience and film-makers on the same level and establish dramatic irony.

This example kind of reminds me of Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me. Not that that film is really a fourth wall break, but in the way you describe it re-ambiguating.

That film happened after David Lynch had little to no involvement in Season 2, and the show had from his perspective gone off the rails. What was initially a very intimate story about violence in the family had turned into a sort of ghost-story which absolved a lot of the true horror of the show. Fans of the show had grown attached to this almost “monster of the week” format where we were continuously getting more and more clues to solve the “puzzle” but the show was never made to be a solvable puzzle.

So when David came back on to make a film for the show, fans expected it to continue the story and solve loose ends. And what it ended up being, is this brutally horrific film showing plot events which the community already knew.

It’s like the filmmakers saying, “Hey guys, I know you’re having fun with this, but you’ve missed the forest for the trees.” In Twin Peaks: FWWM, fans missed the themes of patriarchal family violence for the superficial story of the nature of the supernatural. In Blade Runner, fans got so caught up in the mystery of whether or not he’s a replicant, that the deep existential horror of that ambiguity is lost.
  - [I wonder if the dog character might have been inspired by a certain interview with Brando](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=fgvMPUcw_2Q)
2. My favorite, which in a way gives a further twist to the whole concept of a 4th wall, is from the only film written and directed by master cinematographer Haskell Wexler, [Medium Cool](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=MydIOnH5ac0) (1968). 

Wexler started out wanting to make a film about Appalachian migrants to Chicago, but wound up in the middle of the police and protesters’ riots at the 1968 Democratic Convention, and wrote them into the story on the fly.

He even got the chance to film the police preparations for the riots, which included cops acting the part of radical hippies. (So a fiction film includes real cops acting the part of their own imagination of their enemies.) When the actual riots went down, his cinematic instinct to get the shot lead another crew member to audibly remind him that the violence was real. 
  - That sounds so fascinating. Will definitely check this out soon, thanks for the recommendation!
    - I forgot to mention it stars Robert Forster of later Tarantino and Vince Gilligan fame. 
3. There’s a film by >!Nuri Bilge Ceylan!< with a massive “why did they do that?” conversation starter of a technical fourth wall break. The curious thing about this moment is that the film takes it in its stride. It doesn’t have any impact on the momentum and tendency of the story. It can be shrugged off, basically, by viewers who are just interested in the story, as soon as it has passed. Nevertheless it can also come off as a perfectly judged flourish by the director. I don’t even want to say what the film is though. I think that you don’t want to be waiting around while watching one of these films for a fourth wall breaking moment.

I also actually happened to write in another post today 

>Recently I’ve begun to speculate whether the film [Sentimental Value] might be improved by having some extra framing device. For instance, Bergman Island breaks the distinction between the main narrative of the writer on her retreat and the story she’s working on there, by having one of her fictional characters interact with ‘real’ characters in the ending.

I didn’t actually enjoy watching the film Bergman Island at all, and this move it made late in the film was rather more like a nail in its coffin to me. All the same, the thrust of my point about Sentimental Value is that upon reflection, I think there’s something about it that’s bordering on a kind of self-satisfied closure indistinguishable from television. Fourth wall breaking of course is not the only way to avoid that. It probably shouldn’t be the first thing a director reaches for in order to have their story feel less ‘self-satisfied’, but Sentimental Value is a text with films within the film already, so I feel that maybe something of the calling attention to the overall artifice thing of Bergman Island might have felt appropriate.
